speaker: interviewer
text: So we should show this as part of the documents. Do you have any other photos of you? Do you have anything from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Before the war [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a [INAUDIBLE]. It looks familiar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I was talking about my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: --about the brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. Watch your step.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the speech when I made [INAUDIBLE] club.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 26, 1995. We're speaking with Mr. Sam Zelikson. I'm Leslie Fass. We're in Mount Kisco, New York. Language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, one more time. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 26, 1995. We're speaking with Mr. Sam Zelikson. I'm Leslie Fass in Mount Kisco, New York, USA. We're speaking in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 26, 1995. We're speaking with Mr. Sam Zelikson. Tape one. I'm Leslie Fass in Mount Kisco, New York, USA, in English. Mr. Zelikson, could you give us your full name at birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name at birth is Siky-- Siky, they called me. The-- my students, my friends use to call me 'Sik-eh'.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how you spell your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's S-I-K-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zelikson, Z-E-L-I-K-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: July the 1, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Viski.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Viski, Latvia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Latvia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that where you were raised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Raised, actually I was in Dvinsk, because my father had an iron business, my grandfather and my father. And the iron business got too big in Viski, so we moved to Dvinsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know how old you were when you moved to Dvinsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was exactly 10 years old?

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said your father had an iron business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a particular name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zelikson Iron.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: A housewife. She used to do volunteer work to help people to invite poor people for supper on Friday night. And she used to do a lot of charity work--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --help out in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham in Jewish, Abba in English, Izzy in English, Ichock in Jewish, and Joseph in English, and Josl in Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they older or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was older, four years older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abba. We called him Abe Tevi. He was 16 when the Nazis moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in 1941?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Izzy, Izzy was 10 years old. And Joseph was eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're saying 10 and 8 respectively in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1941, you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 years old. What type of education did you have, up to that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had up to the sixth grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend public school or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what a typical day of school was like for you, going through the public school day as well as the [HEBREW], the time and the hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the public school, we used to go 8 o'clock in the morning to about 3 o'clock in the afternoon. Then I went. We-- we ate, and we played. And 6 o'clock, I had to go to [HEBREW] from 6:00 to 9:00.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you attended public school, was there any religious education within the school as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was strictly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Strictly public school. They taught us, of course, our language Latvish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when the Russian came in, we had to learn Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: But before 1940, when you were studying in the public school, did you ever experience any anti-Semitism among your gentile friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe an incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they used to call you dirty Jew. You're too lazy. You don't like to work. You like to do a lot of business. But you don't like to work. But we told them, we were only students not trying to be better than anybody else, just studying. And they didn't like it. They-- they say we're too smart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- were these incidents ever in front of a teacher in your public school? Or, was this after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, during play.

speaker: interviewer
text: During play?

speaker: interviewee
text: During play.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever physically harm you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Verbal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Verbal anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have gentile friends that you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they ever make remarks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not my friends, no. We were good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they live near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your neighborhood like? Did you live in a house, an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no. We lived in-- we had an apartment house. In Viski, we lived in a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had like a ranch house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, there was no street, because it was a small town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And everything was fine. And when we moved to Dvinsk, my father bought a big apartment house. And we were living downstairs. And we had tenants.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living in Viski, could you describe what your house looked like? Did you and your brothers share a room? Did everyone have their own room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had-- we had our own rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have toilet facilities in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it further, your dining room, or a study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had a study room. We had a big dining room. We had a table. You could sit 12 people, like a conference room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have visitors come to your home very often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a Jewish holiday celebration in your home in Viski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, Rosh Hashanna, Yom Kippur, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and Purim, the most happiest [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a Passover Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had-- oh, we used to have at least-- every time we had a Passover, we had about 30, 40 people for the Seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were-- where they all relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some relatives, some neighbors, some friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe the table setting? What was on the table?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of course, first, it was covered with table cloth, beautiful linen table cloth. We had the [NON-ENGLISH]. All the forks, knives, and everything was silver, gold plated. And the plate was from China. I think it was Rosenthal china. And, of course, Passover was when everything was displayed, the most expensive articles.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we had to drink wine from-- not from a glass, but from a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH], it means like a-- I can't describe it in the-- in the English. It's a silver [? chalice. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a goblet?

speaker: interviewee
text: A goblet, yeah. That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone drank from the same way or they had their own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, each had separate. My father had a big one. And my mother had the second largest. And the children had all the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said what was this made out of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Silver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there inscriptions on in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, right now, I can't remember exactly. But I know it had a beautiful line on it. It had a Bible on it. And it said, [NON-ENGLISH] on the goblets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who prepared the meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your brothers help at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, because we was small. But we had enough help, because we had a woman taking care [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the household.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would describe Passover as a festive occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the main course that you ate for Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a [INAUDIBLE]. We had chicken. We had fish, gefilte fish. We had herring. Of course, we had all kinds of cakes. And, of course, matzhas. And everything was kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the matzah hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, covered with a piece of cloth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the children go to look for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after we said the four [INAUDIBLE]. When we said [INAUDIBLE], the youngest went to look. And my father used to hide it in his pants. On this side of his pants, he used to hide it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we found it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which was your youngest brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Joseph would find it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He'd find it. My father used to give him-- used to give him-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. That's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] equal in American money maybe a penny.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Joseph must have been thrilled with this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It wasn't the money. It was the thrill of finding it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you moved to Dvinsk--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was that a happy move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe your home there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Over there, we bought a big apartment. The building was four floors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we liked it very much. It wasn't like a private-- your house. Of course, it belonged to my father and my grandfather. But I liked at Viski better, because it was much larger, the house. And it didn't look that ugly. The apartment house looked not so nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandparents live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these your paternal grandparents? Your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your mother's parents alive at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in Viski. They stayed in the house. They didn't want to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what languages were spoken in your home when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish and Latvish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents speak any other languages--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that the children wouldn't understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father spoke fluent German. He also spoke fluent Dutch. He also spoke Swiss and Hebrew, and also fluent Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother used to speak Russian, Latvish, Jewish, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and a little German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what languages did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, I spoke Latvish and Jewish. But in 1940, when we started to go to school when the Russians came in, we had to learn Russian. They didn't want us to speak Latvish. They wanted to speak Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of literature was found in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had all kind of literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Newspapers, books?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had books. We had like a library.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a private library in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you recall what languages the books or newspapers may have been in?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot in Hebrew. Of course, we had a lot of Lettish books. There was Hebrew. There was some [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish, beautiful [NON-ENGLISH] works. And there was quite a few in Latvish and Russian and also German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- was your family culturally involved? Did you go to plays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you went to your first play that you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father took us on a Jewish play, [NON-ENGLISH]. When in-- it was in 1940 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the whole family went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a big occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or, was it something you frequently did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. For the kids, it was a big occasion. My father and mother used to go. And the nanny used to take care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would you describe your home as a well-to-do home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother had help in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family considered-- do you consider your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you often go to shul on Saturdays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. There's no-- not on Saturday. I had to go Friday night, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Friday night and Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any awareness of the oncoming war and what your parents was talking about in reference to Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we didn't even know anything about Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: We meaning the children, your brothers, or we meaning--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents? Do you think they knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the parents never spoke to us about war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you ever tell if, in the late '30s, if they seemed apprehensive or had--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father used to do a lot of business. He used to go at least once a month in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Berlin, Hamburg. He used to go buy the iron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Beerfelden, he used to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once a month. But you don't recall any conversations your parents may have had that seemed in another language or apprehensive or very--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't speak to us any kind of politics. They didn't discuss with us. They were interested in our education. But as far as politics, it was never spoken.

speaker: interviewer
text: So up to the age of 12, did you have any awareness of the oncoming war, of Hitler, of Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, before I was in the end of 1940, that's where the Germans moved in. I was in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dvinsk. I was in the street. And I heard boom, boom, boom, boom. And I says to a friend of mine, what goes on? There just big explosion.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, don't you know? They're-- they're bombing our city. I saw the buildings falling.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't believe it. So I ran home. And I told my parents that the building was falling. They says, I'm dreaming. It's not so. Then when my father went out, he saw it. So I went in the street to look. And as I was looking in the street, I saw a German. They come down from the airplanes with the parachutes. And he said to me, [GERMAN], come over here. [GERMAN] in German means young. So I came over.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, are you Jewish? Are you a [GERMAN]? And I looked at him, I said, yeah. He said, come with me. So I didn't want to go. I started to run. He picked up the rifle. And he started to shoot at me. Lucky I was fast enough, and I escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go back to where my house. I saw there was a truck. And they were pushing out my father and my mother and my three brothers. And they moved-- moved them. I didn't even know where to go. And I didn't want-- I was afraid to go back home. So the first thing I thought about it, I'm going to run away. But I didn't know where to run. So not too far from us was the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was happened when the German moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: They moved in in June. Beginning of June they moved in in 1941. So I didn't know where to hide. So I figured the safest place would be in cemetery. So I went in the cemetery. And I slept over there. But at night, the wind was blowing. And the trees moved. And it start to make a noise like woo, woo. And when I saw it, I thought that people were dancing in front of me. And I got scared and tried to sleep, but I couldn't sleep. And then for a couple of days, I didn't eat nothing. And I was starving. I didn't have nothing to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started to dig the grave. And as I dug with my hand, I find a bone. And I was so hungry, I started chew on the bone. And then I realized it was from the dead people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time I didn't even think about, because they say hunger breaks iron. And I chewed on the bone. And that was in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] middle of June. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I tried to go in the farm to find if I can find a raw potato or a carrot. And somebody saw me. And they must have told the Latvian police. So I ran back to the cemetery. And I was there about six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no contact with anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And after six months, I started to go to look, see if I can find somebody. But a woman saw me. And she started screaming you dirty Jew. And she told the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] was the Latvian soldier. And they took me. And they sent me back to Dvinsk ghetto. In Dvinsk ghetto, I find my father, my brothers, my mother. But my mother was separate. And my father was separate. And my brothers were separate. I couldn't go from one to the other, because each one of the blocks was separate. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which members of your family were together, if any?

speaker: interviewee
text: My three brothers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were together?

speaker: interviewee
text: --were together. My mother was separate. And my father was separate. And when they brought me in Dvinsk ghetto, that's-- it started to get cold. It must have been, I would say, maybe the end of November. And the German, the guard, was watching the people. He grabbed me by the arm and says, come over here. Light a fire for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I says to him, I don't know how to light a fire. I never lit a fire. He says, I'll show you. Just do it, what I tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took two bricks. He-- he showed me how to put the two bricks with some wood and to light a match and light a fire. But I wasn't fast enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took the boot, and he kicked me. And my father stood next to me. And he goes over to the German and says, you know, you kicked my son for no reason. Why did you do it? So the German didn't say nothing. He took off the rifle. And he shot my father right in front of me. And at that moment, I-- I couldn't cry. I-- I was completely stunned.

speaker: interviewee
text: I froze. I wanted to cry, but I couldn't. I just, my eyes went like this. And I couldn't move. And my father was right in front of me, dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I was working for the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And excuse me, you were 12 and 1/2 at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I was working for the German to take care of him, to heat him up, take off his boots. And an officer came. And he says, I need you to go to my house and to clean my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I says to him, I can't do it by myself. He says, do you know a woman? I says yes, I know a woman. She's right here. And that was my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, OK, bring her. And I find out in which block my mother was. And I went with my mother to clean. Of course, it was a Jewish house, but he says it was his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you.
